presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: Severe headache and high fever with neck stiffness
I can't touch my chin to my chest — my neck hurts too much
Worst headache ever with fever and sensitivity to light
High fever, terrible headache, and my neck is very stiff
Severe headache with fever and nausea — light is killing me

history_data.hpi: Patient developed a severe generalized headache 20 hours ago, followed by high fever and progressive neck stiffness. Associated with photophobia, nausea, and multiple episodes of vomiting. Symptoms have rapidly worsened over the past 6 hours. Denies recent head trauma or travel.
patient_quotes: This is the worst headache I've ever had in my life.
I can't bend my neck at all — it's killing me.
The lights are making everything so much worse.
I feel absolutely feverish and sick.

core_facts.neck_stiffness_severity: unable to flex chin to chest

investigations.CT Head: No acute intracranial pathology. No mass effect. No hydrocephalus. No herniation. Normal ventricular size. Imaging does not contraindicate lumbar puncture.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 16.8 × 10⁹/L (markedly elevated with left shift), Hb 13.2 g/dL, Platelets 245 × 10⁹/L. Leukocytosis with predominance of neutrophils consistent with acute bacterial infection.
investigations.CRP: 187 mg/L (markedly elevated) — confirms acute systemic inflammation
investigations.ESR: 68 mm/hr (markedly elevated) — consistent with acute bacterial meningitis
investigations.Blood Culture: Pending at 6 hours; empiric antibiotics already started. Culture positive for Neisseria meningitidis at 24 hours (later confirmed).
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 128 mmol/L (hyponatraemia — SIADH from meningitis), K 3.9 mmol/L, Creatinine 89 µmol/L, Urea 8.2 mmol/L. Hyponatraemia is critical finding warranting fluid restriction.
investigations.Lumbar Puncture (CSF): Opening pressure elevated at 32 cmH₂O. CSF: cloudy/turbid appearance, protein 1.8 g/L (markedly elevated), glucose 1.1 mmol/L (low, CSF:serum ratio 0.2), WBC 2400 cells/µL (predominantly neutrophils 94%), RBC 45 cells/µL. Gram stain: Gram-negative diplococci. Bacterial antigen testing positive for Neisseria meningitidis.

essential_examinations: meningeal signs — Kernig and Brudzinski signs
critical_actions: start empiric IV antibiotics immediately — do not delay for lumbar puncture
arrange urgent lumbar puncture if no contraindications
management_actions: Start empiric IV antibiotics without delay — ceftriaxone 2g IV 6-hourly + vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV 8-hourly + ampicillin 2g IV 4-hourly
CT head if any contraindication to lumbar puncture
Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis, culture, and sensitivities
Fluid restriction for hyponatraemia secondary to SIADH
Identify and prophylax close contacts — rifampicin or ceftriaxone
Repeat LP if no clinical improvement in 48 hours
critical_history_questions: When exactly did the headache start?
Have you had fever? How high?
Do you have neck stiffness — can you touch your chin to your chest?
Are bright lights painful to look at?
Do you live in shared accommodation?
